title: Authentication strength indicators
text: Evaluate whether authentication mechanisms resist common weaknesses such as default credentials, weak password policies, or absence of account lockout. The presence of a login page alone does not indicate strong security.

title: Authentication evidence requirements
text: Before concluding authentication is secure, verify actual enforcement mechanisms such as credential strength, rate limiting, and session protection. Superficial indicators like login forms are insufficient evidence.

title: Transport security implications
text: If the web interface uses unencrypted HTTP, credentials and session tokens may be exposed to local attackers. This directly weakens trust boundaries between user and device.

title: Configuration exposure reasoning
text: Administrative interfaces should be restricted and protected. If configuration endpoints are accessible without strong controls, this increases risk of unauthorized modification.

title: Evidence-based vulnerability reasoning
text: Security conclusions should be based on observed evidence such as authentication behavior, encryption usage, and access restrictions, rather than assumptions based on interface presence.

title: Trust boundary analysis for web UI
text: A device web interface represents a trust boundary between user and system. Weak authentication or unencrypted communication reduces this boundary and increases exposure risk.

title: Remediation for web interface issues
text: Typical mitigations include enforcing strong authentication, eliminating default credentials, enabling HTTPS, and restricting administrative access.

title: Service exposure implications
text: Exposed services should be evaluated based on necessity and access control. Unnecessary services expand the attack surface and increase potential entry points.

title: Trust boundary in local network
text: Local network exposure should not be assumed safe. Devices must enforce proper authentication even within internal networks.

title: MQTT broker authentication reasoning
text: If an MQTT broker accepts unauthenticated connections on the local network, unauthorized entities may observe or influence device communications.

title: MQTT topic exposure risks
text: Sensitive topics such as telemetry, status, or control messages should be protected. Unrestricted topic access can lead to data leakage or unauthorized control.

title: Message-level trust reasoning
text: Even if communication is functional, lack of authentication or authorization at message level weakens system trust boundaries.

title: MQTT remediation themes
text: Mitigations include broker authentication, topic-level access control, network segmentation, and encryption where applicable.

title: BLE pairing trust assumptions
text: BLE pairing should not assume nearby devices are trustworthy. Weak pairing mechanisms can allow unauthorized connections.

title: Proximity threat reasoning
text: Physical proximity does not guarantee security. Attackers within range may exploit weak wireless protections.

title: BLE data exposure risks
text: If pairing or communication lacks protection, sensitive data may be exposed to unauthorized nearby devices.

title: Wireless trust boundary
text: Wireless interfaces introduce additional trust boundaries where authentication and encryption must be enforced.

title: BLE mitigation strategies
text: Secure pairing mechanisms, encryption, and device authentication are key to reducing wireless attack surface risks.

title: Firmware integrity importance
text: Firmware updates must be verified using signatures or integrity checks to prevent unauthorized modification.

title: Authenticity vs integrity
text: Integrity ensures data is unchanged, while authenticity ensures it comes from a trusted source. Both are required for secure firmware updates.

title: Update channel trust
text: Even valid firmware can be compromised if delivered through an untrusted or insecure update channel.

title: Firmware remediation
text: Secure updates require signed firmware, verified delivery channels, and protection against rollback attacks.

title: Reporting justification guidance
text: When reporting a vulnerability, clearly connect observed evidence to security impact and avoid unsupported assumptions.

title: Avoiding overstatement
text: Do not claim full compromise unless supported by evidence. Distinguish between observed weaknesses and inferred risks.

title: Remediation explanation clarity
text: Effective remediation should explain both what to fix and why the issue introduces risk within the system.

title: Broker exposure does not imply safe internal trust
text: A service being reachable only on an internal network does not make it safe by default. Internal services still require authentication and authorization controls.

title: Unsigned update reasoning
text: If a firmware update process lacks signature verification, the learner should reason about authenticity failure rather than only general software risk.

title: Pairing evidence requirements
text: Before concluding BLE pairing is secure, verify how device identity is established, whether user confirmation is meaningful, and whether repeated pairing attempts are controlled.

title: Login page misconception correction
text: A visible authentication screen may indicate an access boundary, but it does not by itself prove that credentials are strong, sessions are protected, or access control is effective.

title: Telemetry sensitivity reasoning
text: Telemetry may appear harmless, but it can reveal device state, behavior patterns, or operational details that become security-relevant when broadly exposed.
